report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Priscila, thanks for sharing all of this with us. Your answers paint a really clear picture, and your instinct not to drop your calories even lower is spot on. Here's what we're seeing.

### Your Plateau Is Metabolic, Not Motivational

You've done months of consistent work in a calorie deficit, and it paid off for a while. But now your body has adapted. This is one of the most well-documented phenomena in metabolism: [prolonged caloric restriction causes a sustained reduction in T3, the active thyroid hormone that drives your metabolic rate](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/16720655/). Your body literally slows down how much energy it burns to match the reduced intake.

What makes your situation especially clear is that you've layered multiple metabolic suppressors on top of each other over time: calorie counting, keto/low-carb phases, intermittent fasting, and intense exercise. Each of these independently pushes the same lever:

```
Caloric restriction ──→ ↓ T3 (active thyroid hormone)
Low-carb dieting ────→ ↓ T3 + ↑ Reverse T3 (blocks T3)
Fasting ─────────────→ ↓ T3 + ↑ stress hormones
Intense exercise ────→ ↑ cortisol + ↑ energy demand
                              ↓
                    Metabolic rate suppression
                              ↓
                       Weight loss plateau
```

Research shows that [low-carbohydrate diets specifically reduce T3 and increase reverse T3, even independent of total calories](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1249190/). And [caloric restriction lowers T3 due to the restriction itself, not just fat loss](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2649744/). So even though you're "doing everything right," your metabolism has downshifted to conserve energy.

Your moderate energy level (6/10) fits this picture perfectly. Not crashed, but not thriving either.

### Your Diet May Be Under-Fueling the Recovery

Looking at what you eat day to day, a few things stand out. Your protein intake is solid, but your diet appears quite low in carbohydrates and overall calories for someone training hard. Eggs, lean meat, veggies, and fruit are all quality foods, but the total energy and carbohydrate content may not be enough to signal to your body that it's safe to keep your metabolic rate up.

Carbohydrates play a direct role in T3 production. Without enough of them, the body favors the inactive form of thyroid hormone (reverse T3), which effectively puts the brakes on your metabolism. Your body reads low fuel availability as a reason to conserve, not burn.

### Your Digestive Symptoms Add Another Layer

You mentioned digestive issues, which is worth paying attention to here. When energy metabolism is suppressed, so is digestive function: stomach acid, enzyme secretion, and gut motility all require adequate thyroid hormone and metabolic energy. This can create a cycle where poor digestion limits nutrient absorption, which further impairs the metabolism that digestion depends on.

```
Suppressed metabolism ──→ Weakened digestion
         ↑                        ↓
         └── Poor nutrient absorption
```

---

Your instinct is right, Priscila. Eating less is not the answer here. The patterns in your answers suggest this is a metabolic and hormonal picture, not a discipline problem. A quiz like this can only surface so much, but the connections between your plateau, your dieting history, your energy level, and your digestion are consistent and worth exploring further.
